personaIntros.tutor: I want you to act as a software testing teacher
personaIntros.student: I want you to act as a fellow student learning software testing
personaIntros.professional: I want you to act as an experienced software engineer with a strong background in TDD and software quality

games.title: Red or Green?
games.description: Sharpen your instinct for spotting good and bad tests

steps.title: Part 1: Setup
setup: Copy and paste the following prompt:

steps.title: Part 2: Analyse the Test
guidelines: Give your verdict before asking for hints — is this test actually useful?
Think about what the test does NOT cover before the AI explains
Ask yourself: would this test catch a real bug, or would it always pass?

games.title: Write the Test First
games.description: Practice TDD by writing tests before any implementation exists

steps.title: Part 1: Setup
setup: Copy and paste the following prompt:

steps.title: Part 2: Write Your Tests
guidelines: Write tests in pseudocode — no specific programming language needed
Each test should check ONE behaviour only
Ask yourself: if this feature is not implemented, will my test be RED?
Aim for at least one edge case test per acceptance criterion

personaIntros.tutor: I want you to act as a software design teacher
personaIntros.student: I want you to act as a fellow student learning object-oriented design
personaIntros.professional: I want you to act as an experienced software architect with deep knowledge of OO design principles

games.title: Fix the Design
games.description: Spot and fix intentional design flaws in a UML class diagram

steps.title: Part 1: Setup
setup: Copy and paste the following prompt:

steps.title: Part 2: Find the Flaws
guidelines: Copy the diagram from the Selected Context below and paste it into your LLM chat
State which relationship or class you think is wrong before asking for hints
Name the design principle being violated — don't just say 'it looks wrong'
Propose a concrete fix for each flaw you find

games.title: SOLID Violations
games.description: Identify which SOLID principles are broken and explain how to fix them

steps.title: Part 1: Setup
setup: Copy and paste the following prompt:

steps.title: Part 2: Find the Violations
guidelines: Copy the class description from the Selected Context below and paste it into your LLM chat
Name the specific SOLID principle being violated — S, O, L, I, or D
Explain what change or scenario would expose the problem before asking for hints
Propose a concrete refactoring or structural fix for each violation

games.title: Sequence Diagram Repair
games.description: Spot flaws in UML sequence diagrams — wrong order, layer bypasses, missing flows

steps.title: Part 1: Setup
setup: Copy and paste the following prompt:

steps.title: Part 2: Find the Flaws
guidelines: Copy the diagram from the Selected Context below and paste it into your LLM chat
Describe which message or participant you think is wrong before asking for hints
Think about message order, layer boundaries, and missing alt blocks
Propose a corrected version of the problematic messages

personaIntros.tutor: I want you to act as a software engineering teacher
personaIntros.student: I want you to act as a fellow student learning software engineering best practices
personaIntros.professional: I want you to act as a senior software engineer

problems.name: Food Sharing App
problems.statement: People in food poverty need access to fresh food in a way that preserves their dignity and avoids excessive bureaucracy.
problems.description: An app that helps people locate fresh produce in their area more easily. Allotment owners can advertise their excess produce and when/how it can be picked up. Registered users can browse and check out the produce they like.

personas.name: Sarah
personas.description: A young mother of two who has recently lost her job. She believes buying frozen or fast food is not a matter of ignorance but of being unable to afford healthy food. She doesn't want to use a food bank because she doesn't want to be seen asking for help.

personas.name: Benjamin
personas.description: Owns an allotment and has been overproducing. Wants to transform part of his allotment to provide those on benefits, pensioners on state pension and those on a low-income free organic fruit and vegetables. But doesn't know how to reach those people.

userStories.statement: As an allotment user, I want to create an app account so that I can save my preferences
userStories.description: User registration for accessing fresh produce listings and preferences

acceptanceCriteria.criteria: Given that the user is on the register page, when the user enters email, password, types of fresh food, and locations to pick up the food from, then the user should be registered and directed to the login page
Given that the user entered a password that doesn't meet password requirements, when the registration button is clicked, then the error message 'invalid password' is displayed
Given that the user entered an invalid email, when the registration button is clicked, then the error message 'invalid email' is displayed

problems.name: Reset Password
problems.statement:
You are given the main flow of a use case for resetting a forgotten password. 
 Identify the flaws and inaccuracies in the steps.
problems.description:
Primary Actor: Registered User

Current Main Flow (FLAWED - find the issues!):
1. User clicks reset password
2. System sends them the link
3. User enters new password
4. System updates the account
problems.note: This exercise focuses on analyzing the MAIN FLOW of a use case. A complete use case would also include preconditions, postconditions, and alternative flows.

problems.name: User Login
problems.statement: You are given the main flow of a use case for logging in to an account. Identify the flaws and inaccuracies in the steps.
problems.description:
Primary Actor: Registered User

Current Main Flow (FLAWED - find the issues!):
1. User enters their username
2. User submits the form
3. System grants access to the dashboard
problems.note: This exercise focuses on analyzing the MAIN FLOW of a use case. A complete use case would also include preconditions, postconditions, and alternative flows.

problems.name: Book a Meeting Room
problems.statement: You are given the main flow of a use case for booking a meeting room. Identify the flaws and inaccuracies in the steps.
problems.description:
Primary Actor: Employee

Current Main Flow (FLAWED - find the issues!):
1. Employee selects a room
2. Employee chooses a date
3. System books the room
4. Employee receives a confirmation
problems.note: This exercise focuses on analyzing the MAIN FLOW of a use case. A complete use case would also include preconditions, postconditions, and alternative flows.

problems.name: isPalindrome — happy path only
problems.statement: Tests isPalindrome with a single positive case ('racecar'). No negative case or edge cases are included.
problems.description:
System (pseudocode):
function isPalindrome(s):
  return s.toLowerCase() == s.toLowerCase().reversed()

Test under analysis:
test isPalindrome_givenKnownPalindrome_returnsTrue:
  assert isPalindrome('racecar') == true

This is the only test written for isPalindrome.

problems.name: reverseString — trivial assertions
problems.statement: Tests reverseString by checking the result is non-null and has the same length as the input. The actual reversal is never verified.
problems.description:
System (pseudocode):
function reverseString(s):
  return s.split('').reverse().join('')

Test under analysis:
test reverseString_returnsResult:
  result = reverseString('hello')
  assert result != null
  assert result.length == 5

problems.name: isPalindrome — five cases in one test
problems.statement: Tests isPalindrome by asserting five different cases inside a single test function.
problems.description:
System (pseudocode):
function isPalindrome(s):
  return s.toLowerCase() == s.toLowerCase().reversed()

Test under analysis:
test isPalindrome_handlesAllCases:
  assert isPalindrome('racecar') == true
  assert isPalindrome('hello') == false
  assert isPalindrome('') == true
  assert isPalindrome('A') == true
  assert isPalindrome('Racecar') == true

problems.name: addItem — asserts on internal state
problems.statement: Tests addItem by directly inspecting the cart's internal items dictionary rather than using the public API.
problems.description:
System (pseudocode):
class ShoppingCart:
  items = {}   // dictionary: name -> price
  function addItem(name, price): items[name] = price
  function removeItem(name): delete items[name]
  function getTotal(): return sum(items.values())

Test under analysis:
test addItem_addsTwoItems:
  cart = ShoppingCart()
  cart.addItem('apple', 1.00)
  cart.addItem('bread', 2.50)
  assert cart.items['apple'] == 1.00
  assert cart.items['bread'] == 2.50
  assert len(cart.items) == 2

problems.name: removeItem — relies on getTotal to verify
problems.statement: Tests removeItem by checking the cart total drops to zero, rather than directly verifying the item was removed.
problems.description:
System (pseudocode):
class ShoppingCart:
  items = {}   // dictionary: name -> price
  function addItem(name, price): items[name] = price
  function removeItem(name): delete items[name]
  function getTotal(): return sum(items.values())

Test under analysis:
test removeItem_removesItem:
  cart = ShoppingCart()
  cart.addItem('apple', 1.00)
  cart.removeItem('apple')
  assert cart.getTotal() == 0

problems.name: Bank Account
problems.statement: A BankAccount class needs to be built from scratch using TDD. No implementation exists yet — your tests define the specification. The account supports deposits and withdrawals; overdrafts are not permitted.
problems.description: Write tests in pseudocode. Focus on specifying behaviour, not implementation. Use descriptive test names following the pattern: test [method]_[condition]_[expectedResult]
problems.context: TDD: write the test first, then implement the minimum code to make it pass, then refactor.

personas.name: Alex
personas.description: Will implement BankAccount using your tests as the specification. They will write only the minimum code needed to make each test pass.

userStories.statement: As a bank customer, I want to deposit money into my account so that my balance increases.
userStories.description: The account starts with a zero balance. Multiple deposits should accumulate.

acceptanceCriteria.criteria: Depositing a positive amount increases the balance by that exact amount
Multiple deposits accumulate correctly
Depositing zero does not change the balance
Depositing a negative amount is rejected

userStories.statement: As a bank customer, I want to withdraw money from my account so that I can access my funds.

acceptanceCriteria.criteria: Withdrawing an amount less than the balance decreases the balance correctly
Withdrawing exactly the full balance results in a zero balance
Withdrawing more than the balance is rejected (no overdraft)
Withdrawing a negative amount is rejected

problems.name: Password Validator
problems.statement: A PasswordValidator needs to be built from scratch using TDD. No implementation exists yet — your tests define the specification. It validates whether a password meets security requirements.
problems.description: Write tests in pseudocode. The validator returns true for valid passwords and false (or raises an error) for invalid ones.
problems.context: Password validation is a common security requirement. Boundary values matter here — students often miss the 'exactly 8 characters' case.

userStories.statement: As a user registering an account, I want clear feedback if my password is too short so that I know why my registration failed.

acceptanceCriteria.criteria: A password with fewer than 8 characters is invalid
A password with exactly 8 characters satisfies the length requirement
An empty string is invalid

userStories.statement: As a security administrator, I want passwords to contain a mix of character types so that accounts are resistant to brute-force attacks.

acceptanceCriteria.criteria: A password must contain at least one uppercase letter
A password must contain at least one digit
A password that meets both length and complexity rules is valid
A password that meets length but fails complexity rules is invalid

problems.name: Mental Health Assistance App
problems.statement: Young adults aged 18–24 experiencing mental health difficulties need accessible, affordable, and stigma-free support in a way that respects their privacy and removes barriers to seeking help.
problems.description: An app that tracks users' mood over time and provides personalised resources, peer group chat, and volunteer-led one-to-one support for young adults aged 18–24 who may not otherwise seek professional help.

personas.name: Stephany
personas.description: A 20-year-old university student who experiences anxiety, low mood, and loneliness. She feels pressure from academic life, compares herself to others on social media, and struggles with self-esteem. She would not visit a GP or therapist due to cost, waiting times, and fear of judgment, but she is open to anonymous digital support that she can access privately on her phone.

personas.name: Marcus
personas.description: A 34-year-old qualified counsellor who volunteers a few hours a week to support young people who would never walk into a therapy room. He wants a simple, moderated interface for conducting anonymous one-to-one text sessions without excessive administrative burden.

userStories.statement: As a user, I want to be given advice on how to feel better through personal goals and access my previously achieved goals to view my progress
userStories.description: Personal goal setting and progress tracking for mental health improvement

acceptanceCriteria.criteria: Given that the user has completed a mood check-in, when the check-in is submitted, the app should display a set of personalised goal suggestions based on the user's current mood score
Given that the user is viewing their suggested goals, when the user selects a goal and taps 'Set as my goal', the goal should be saved and displayed on the user's goals dashboard as active
Given that the user has marked a goal as complete, when the user navigates to the progress section, the completed goal should appear in a 'Goals achieved' list with the date it was completed
Given that the user has no previously completed goals, when the user navigates to the progress section, the message 'No goals completed yet — keep going!' should be displayed
Given that the user taps on a previously achieved goal, when the goal detail view opens, the user should be shown the goal description and the date it was completed

problems.name: Invoice Manager
problems.statement: A class description for an InvoiceManager has been written. It compiles and the developer says it does everything needed — but it contains SOLID violations that will make it hard to maintain.
problems.description:
Class: InvoiceManager
Responsibilities as described by the developer:
- Holds a list of line items (product name, quantity, unit price)
- addItem(name, quantity, price): adds a line item
- calculateTotal(): sums all line items and applies a 10% discount if total > 100
- formatAsPDF(): renders the invoice layout and returns a PDF file
- saveToDatabase(connectionString): opens a DB connection and persists the invoice
- sendByEmail(recipientAddress): connects to an SMTP server and emails the PDF to the customer

All five methods are on the same class.
problems.context: Two SOLID principles are violated here. Think about how many different reasons this class has to change, and what would happen if the company switched from MySQL to PostgreSQL, or from SMTP to SendGrid.

problems.name: Bird Hierarchy
problems.statement: A class hierarchy models birds in a nature simulation. It looks clean at first glance — but substituting one subclass for another breaks the simulation. Find the violations.
problems.description:
Class: Bird (base class)
- name: String
- fly(): void  — moves the bird upward in the simulation
- eat(): void
- sleep(): void

Class: Eagle extends Bird
- fly(): void  — soars to high altitude, works as expected

Class: Penguin extends Bird
- fly(): void  — throws UnsupportedOperationException("Penguins cannot fly")

Usage in simulation:
function makeBirdFly(bird: Bird):
  bird.fly()  // called for all birds in the flock
problems.context: One SOLID principle is violated. Think about what happens when the simulation calls makeBirdFly() with a Penguin. Is a Penguin truly substitutable for a Bird in all contexts?

problems.name: Order Service
problems.statement: A class description for an OrderService shows how it places orders. It works today with MySQL and email — but the team plans to support multiple databases and notification channels. Spot what will make that painful.
problems.description:
Class: OrderService
Fields:
- db: MySQLDatabase  (instantiated with new MySQLDatabase() inside the constructor)
- notifier: EmailNotifier  (instantiated with new EmailNotifier() inside the constructor)

Methods:
- placeOrder(order: Order): void
    1. Validates the order
    2. Calls db.save(order) directly
    3. Calls notifier.sendConfirmation(order) directly
    4. Returns

Note: MySQLDatabase and EmailNotifier are concrete classes, not interfaces.
problems.context: One SOLID principle is violated (with two instances of it). Think about what OrderService would need to change if the team switched to PostgreSQL, or wanted to add SMS notifications alongside email.

problems.name: ATM Withdrawal
problems.statement: A sequence diagram models a cash withdrawal at an ATM. The developer says it covers the happy path — but it contains flaws that would cause problems in a real system.
problems.description:
@startuml
actor Customer
participant ATM
participant Account

Customer -> ATM: insertCard(cardNumber)
ATM -> Customer: requestPIN()
Customer -> ATM: enterPIN(pin)
ATM -> Account: withdraw(amount)
Account -> Customer: showBalance()
ATM -> Customer: dispenseCash()
@enduml
problems.context: Three flaws are hidden in this diagram. Think about which participant should be talking to the Customer, what happens if the PIN is wrong or funds are insufficient, and whether the balance should be shown before or after cash is dispensed.

problems.name: Login Flow
problems.statement: A sequence diagram models a user logging in to a web application. The developer says it captures the full flow — but messages are in the wrong order and a critical path is missing.
problems.description:
@startuml
actor User
participant LoginController
participant UserRepository
participant SessionService

User -> LoginController: login(email, password)
LoginController -> User: redirect("/dashboard")
LoginController -> UserRepository: findByEmail(email)
UserRepository -> LoginController: user
LoginController -> SessionService: createSession(user)
SessionService -> LoginController: sessionToken
@enduml
problems.context: Two flaws are hidden in this diagram. Think about when the redirect should happen relative to the other calls, and what should occur if the credentials are invalid.

problems.name: Order Checkout
problems.statement: A sequence diagram models the checkout flow in an e-commerce system. It looks mostly right — but one participant is calling in the wrong direction, and a return message is missing.
problems.description:
@startuml
actor Customer
participant CheckoutController
participant PaymentService
participant OrderRepository

Customer -> CheckoutController: submitOrder(cart)
CheckoutController -> PaymentService: charge(cart.total)
PaymentService -> CheckoutController: paymentResult
PaymentService -> CheckoutController: updateUI("Payment confirmed")
CheckoutController -> OrderRepository: save(order)
CheckoutController -> Customer: orderConfirmation
@enduml
problems.context: Two flaws are hidden in this diagram. Think about which layer PaymentService belongs to and whether it should be sending UI instructions, and whether every call has a matching return.

problems.name: Library System
problems.statement: A class diagram for a Library system has been drafted. It compiles and the developer says it works — but it contains design flaws that will make it hard to maintain and extend.
problems.context: Three flaws are hidden in this diagram. Hint: think about what responsibilities each class has, and whether Library should know about emails and PDFs at all.

problems.name: Payment Processor
problems.statement: A class diagram for an e-commerce checkout has been drafted. It works for PayPal today, but the team plans to add Stripe and bank transfer next quarter. Spot what will make that painful.
problems.context: Two flaws are hidden in this diagram. Hint: what happens when the team wants to support a second payment provider? Which design principle protects against that kind of change?

problems.name: Animal Hierarchy
problems.statement: A class diagram models a zoo simulation. A junior developer noticed that Dog and Cat both have a speak() method and made Dog extend Cat to 'avoid duplication'. Find everything wrong with this.
problems.context: Three flaws are hidden in this diagram. Hint: is a Dog really a Cat? What should both Dog and Cat share instead? And what does ZooKeeper's method signature say about its flexibility?
